coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic, photothermal, and blood-brain barrier-permeable carbon nanodots: A potent multifunctional scavenger for β-amyloid plaque
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Shao, Xu
Li, Muqiong
Yan, Chaoren
Wang, Chao
Wang, Xin
Guan, Ping
Hu, Xiaoling
Fan, Li
coredata.dc:description: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is associated with amyloid production and buildup in the brain, leading to neurodegeneration. In this study, we used a solvent-thermal technique to produce light-sensitive carbon nanodots (L-CNDs). L-CNDs exhibit outstanding photocatalytic properties, producing singlet oxygen (1O2) under 630 nm irradiation. L-CNDs have a high photothermal conversion efficiency of 68.25 % under 808 nm irradiation, allowing for localized heating and regulation of Aβ aggregation. L-CNDs bind Aβ through hydrophobic interaction and π-π stacking. L-CNDs inhibit Aβ aggregation with efficiencies of 61.08 %, 75.09 %, and 91.72 % at 10 μg·mL−1 in photothermal therapy (PTT), photodynamic therapy (PDT), and PTT/PDT synergistic therapy, respectively. L-CNDs efficiently suppress Aβ misfolding, inhibit fibrillation, and promote disaggregation of mature fibrils. L-CNDs mitigate Aβ-induced cytotoxicity in PC12 and HT22 cells. Interestingly, the data showed that 84.6 % of the L-CNDs could penetrate bEnd.3 cells after 8 h of treatment, demonstrating that they have the capacity to cross the blood-brain barrier (BBB) because of their small size. In vitro investigations have shown that L-CNDs can pass through the BBB. Collectively, our findings reveal a unique technique for treating amyloid disorders using carbon nanodots with significant potential for future studies in this area.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodot
Amyloid
Photooxidation
Photothermal
Blood-brain barrier

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Model of blood-brain barrier in vitro
Aβ 42 aggregation inhibition and fibrils disaggregation
The potential of L-CNDs to break through the BBB

This work was financially supported by the Key Research and Development Program of Shaanxi (2020GY-285, 2022GY-198, and 2023-YBGY-295) and Natural Science Basic Research Program of Shaanxi Province (2023-JC-YB-668).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of L-CNDs. (b) Histogram showing size distribution. (c) AFM imaging of L-CNDs. (d) AFM height dispersion. (e-h) L-CNDs XPS complete spectrum with high-resolution C1s, O1s, and N1s. (i) UV-Vis absorption spectra of L-CNDs. (j) FTIR spectrum of L-CNDs. (k) Raman spectrum of L-CNDs.

(a-c) DPBF was used to detect the generation ability of 1O2 of different L-CND concentrations under 630 nm illumination (0.1 W·cm−2), (a) 5 μg·mL−1, (b) 10 μg·mL−1, and (c) 20 μg·mL−1. (d) Normalized absorbance of DPBF in the presence of L-CNDs under illumination. (e) Measurement of generated 1O2 by L-CNDs under illumination (6 min) using EPR spectroscopy. (f) Temperature elevation of L-CNDs solution under the irradiation of 808 nm laser at 0.6 W·cm−2. (g) Four heating and cooling cycles of 100 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs solution. (h) Temperature variation of 100 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs solution under laser irradiation of 0.6 W·cm−2 808 nm for 10 min, then with laser off. (i) Plot of cooling time as a function of -ln(θ).

(a) DNPH assay to monitor carbonyl content in lysozyme (D, dark; L, laser). (b) Tyrosine inner fluorescence assay of Aβ 42. (c) Corresponding fluorescence intensity at 303 nm of Aβ 42 monomeric samples that were cultured with different L-CND concentrations.

(a-c) Inhibitory effect of L-CNDs (10 μg·mL−1) on 20 μM Aβ 42, (a) ThT kinetic aggregation profiles were used to measure the reduction of Aβ 42 under the presence of 10 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs. (b) Aβ 42 CD spectra with and without 10 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs. (c) TEM images of Aβ 42 co-cultured for 24 h with 10 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs. (d-f) Disaggregation effect for 20 μM Aβ 42 fibrils with 10 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs, (d) ThT kinetics were used to measure Aβ 42 disaggregation in the presence of 10 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs. (e) CD spectra of Aβ 42 fibrils co-cultured for 24 h with 10 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs. (f) TEM images of Aβ 42 fibrils co-cultured for 24 h with 10 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs.

L-CNDs alleviate Aβ 42-induced cytotoxicity. (a) The impact of varying L-CNDs concentrations on reducing Aβ 42-induced cytotoxicity in PC12 cells exposed to lasers. (b) The ability of 10 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs to mitigate cytotoxicity of PC12 cells induced by Aβ 42. (c) The impact of various L-CNDs concentrations on reducing Aβ 42-induced cytotoxicity in HT22 cells exposed to lasers. (d) The ability of 10 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs to mitigate cytotoxicity of HT22 cells induced by Aβ 42. (e) ThT staining to identify amyloid fibrils around HT22 cells.
(a) Endocytosis capacity of bEnd.3 cells was analyzed using flow cytometry. (b) CLSM images of bEnd.3 cells treated with 10 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs for 8 h.

In vitro model of the BBB. (a) Diagram schematic of the in vitro model. (b) The absorbance standard curve of L-CNDs. (c) Transport ratio of L-CNDs through the BBB in vitro at different time points (n=3). (d) HT22 flow cytometric analysis following various time intervals. (e) Fluorescence confocal microscopy images of HT22 cells after various time intervals.
Photooxidative and photothermal carbon nanodots for inhibiting amyloid aggregates and alleviating Aβ 42-induced cytotoxicity.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic, photothermal, and blood-brain barrier-permeable carbon nanodots: A potent multifunctional scavenger for β-amyloid plaque

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University, 127 Youyi Road, Xi'an 710072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University, 127 Youyi Road, Xi'an 710072, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry and Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Air Force Medical University, 169 Changle West Road, Xi'an 710032, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry and Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Air Force Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry and Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Air Force Medical University, 169 Changle West Road, Xi'an 710032, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medicine, Xizang Minzu University, Key Laboratory for Molecular Genetic Mechanisms and Intervention Research on High Altitude Disease of Tibet Autonomous Region, Xianyang, Shaanxi 712082, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Medicine, Xizang Minzu University, Key Laboratory for Molecular Genetic Mechanisms and Intervention Research on High Altitude Disease of Tibet Autonomous Region

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Medicine, Xizang Minzu University, Key Laboratory for Molecular Genetic Mechanisms and Intervention Research on High Altitude Disease of Tibet Autonomous Region, Xianyang, Shaanxi 712082, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is associated with amyloid production and buildup in the brain, leading to neurodegeneration. In this study, we used a solvent-thermal technique to produce light-sensitive carbon nanodots (L-CNDs). L-CNDs exhibit outstanding photocatalytic properties, producing singlet oxygen (1O2) under 630 nm irradiation. L-CNDs have a high photothermal conversion efficiency of 68.25 % under 808 nm irradiation, allowing for localized heating and regulation of Aβ aggregation. L-CNDs bind Aβ through hydrophobic interaction and π-π stacking. L-CNDs inhibit Aβ aggregation with efficiencies of 61.08 %, 75.09 %, and 91.72 % at 10 μg·mL−1 in photothermal therapy (PTT), photodynamic therapy (PDT), and PTT/PDT synergistic therapy, respectively. L-CNDs efficiently suppress Aβ misfolding, inhibit fibrillation, and promote disaggregation of mature fibrils. L-CNDs mitigate Aβ-induced cytotoxicity in PC12 and HT22 cells. Interestingly, the data showed that 84.6 % of the L-CNDs could penetrate bEnd.3 cells after 8 h of treatment, demonstrating that they have the capacity to cross the blood-brain barrier (BBB) because of their small size. In vitro investigations have shown that L-CNDs can pass through the BBB. Collectively, our findings reveal a unique technique for treating amyloid disorders using carbon nanodots with significant potential for future studies in this area.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a chronic and progressive neurological condition that is currently the third leading cause of death in the senior population [1,2]. The global prevalence of AD is increasing, creating a significant public health problem [3,4]. AD is characterized by the abnormal cleavage of amyloid precursor protein (APP) and product misfolding, resulting in the development of amyloid plaques [5,6]. Proteolytic enzymes, such as β-secretase and γ-secretase, deconstruct APP to produce β-amyloid (Aβ), which has 40–42 amino acids [7]. Aβ 42 is the most common peptide discovered in the fibrils and plaques of patients with AD [8]. During the aggregation process, Aβ sequesters metal ions, producing reactive oxygen species clusters and activating amino acid residues. The activated amino acids promote lipid peroxidation, which causes oxidative stress and consequent cellular damage [9,10].

Light-responsive nanomaterials offer a wide range of applications in biology, including antibacterial, antitumor, and bioimaging, owing to their noninvasive and spatiotemporally programmable capabilities [11–14]. In recent years, light-mediated techniques, particularly photo triggered administration and phototherapy, have been used to treat amyloid-related disorders [15–17]. To load curcumin into nanoparticles, Lai et al. used a near-infrared (NIR) absorbing conjugated polymer, PDPP3T-O14, as the photothermal core and 1,2-dipalmitoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine as the NIR stimuli gatekeeper [18]. Under NIR laser irradiation, the encapsulated curcumin was effectively liberated from the non-aggregated nanoparticles, thereby increasing its anti-amyloid activity. Photodynamic treatment (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT) are the two primary components of optical therapies. ROS near Aβ may oxidize neighboring amino acid residues, lowering Aβ-induced cytotoxicity by removing their activating capacity [16]. Chung et al. developed Apta@CDs, a red-light-responsive technology that targets Aβ and inhibits its aggregation [19]. Apta@CDs are effective in targeting Aβ in complicated neurophysiological contexts. Apta@CDs modulated Aβ aggregation by oxidizing its residues when exposed to red light. Light-mediated oxidative effects on Aβ have also been documented for metal organic frameworks (MOFs), hydrogen-bonded organic frameworks (HOFs), gold nanoclusters, and other materials [15,20–22]. Light-mediated oxidation techniques could modulate Aβ aggregation and provide new insights for developing therapeutic approaches for amyloid-related disorders. Furthermore, localized heating can affect protein structure and peptide-peptide interactions and prevent aberrant aggregation [23,24]. Recently, it was discovered that composite nanoparticles composed of methoxy poly(ethylene glycol) and the photothermally linked polymer PDPP exhibited NIR light-responsive characteristics [25]. This NP has been used to generate localized heating under NIR light irradiation, inhibiting Aβ aggregation and disaggregating mature Aβ fibrils. Furthermore, NP's photothermal capability of NPs allows excellent penetration across the blood-brain barrier (BBB) to the site of injury. Song et al. reported the use of Prussian blue for photothermal treatment of AD [26]. We designed a nanoassembly, termed yCDs-Ce6, that achieves PDT and PTT synergy [27]. Nonetheless, the preparation of this component is a somewhat complex process. Consequently, for efficient treatment of AD, it is imperative to rationally design nanomaterials with both PDT and PTT capabilities. Moreover, commercial photosensitizers have elevated singlet oxygen (1O2) output; nevertheless, too high singlet oxygen yield may result in harm to healthy tissue.

There have been reports on the use of carbon materials for amyloid disorders, including graphene, carbon nanotubes, and fullerenes [17,28]. Carbon dots are tiny, have great photoluminescence, and are more biocompatible than other carbon compounds [29–32]. Using a solvothermal technique, we produced light-responsive carbon dots (L-CNDs) in this study that have both photodynamic and photothermal capabilities. Strong absorption peaks were observed at 630 nm for the L-CNDs, which also showed good fluorescence characteristics and minimal cytotoxicity. We investigated Aβ 42 as a target and discovered that under 0.1 W·cm−2 630 nm irradiation, inhibition and disaggregation efficiency were further increased using 1O2. Under 0.6 W·cm−2 808 nm irradiation, L-CNDs showed exceptional photothermal conversion capacity, disrupting the structure of Aβ 42 by localized heating. A 630 nm laser was used for photocatalytic excitation and an 808 nm laser was used for photothermal excitation. L-CNDs demonstrated synergistic photodynamic and photothermal treatment, effectively preventing Aβ misfolding and boosting mature Aβ fibril disaggregation. L-CNDs have minimal toxicity toward PC12 and HT22 cells and can reduce Aβ-induced cellular damage. After 8 h of treatment, 84.6 % of the L-CNDs reached bEnd.3 cells, demonstrating their high capacity for bridging the BBB. The capacity of L-CNDs to traverse the blood-brain barrier was further demonstrated using in vitro BBB models. This study presents a novel technique for treating AD using carbon nanodots.

0.16 g of o-Phenylenediamine and 0.11 g of L-glutamic acid were weighed and transferred into a 50 mL high-pressure reaction vessel. Then, 25 mL of 6.1 mol·L−1 sulfuric acid solution was added. The mixture was heated at 210 °C for 10 h [33]. Following the reaction, a 0.22- μm filter was used to filter the resultant product after the liquid had cooled to 25°C. After pouring into a 1000 Da dialysis bag, the filtrate was dialyzed with deionized water for 72 h. Finally, the material was freeze dried to obtain a powdered state.

For this experiment, 1,3-Diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) was used as a probe to measure 1O2 production, as stated in the literature [34]. A solution containing DPBF and L-CNDs (5, 10, and 20 μg·mL−1) was subjected to radiation using a 0.1 W·cm−2 630 nm laser. The absorbance of the DPBF was measured at 418 nm after different irradiation times. After 540 s of illumination, the UV absorption of DPBF was observed in the control group. The 1O2 quantum yield (φ∆) is measured by monitoring the oxidation of DPBF using a UV–vis spectrophotometer. A standard approach is used to determine the relative quantum yield. Methylene blue (MB, φ∆(std) = 0.52) is used as a standard to determine 1O2 quantum yields [35,36]. In brief, an oxygen-saturated solution of L-CNDs and MB with 20 μg·mL−1 DPBF was produced (in the dark) and irradiated with 630 nm LED light (0.1 W·cm−2). The absorption of DPBF was studied at several time periods. And the quantum yields were estimated using the following equation.
φ
∆
=
φ
∆
std
×
S
x
S
std
×
F
std
F
x
x: L-CNDs
std: MB
S: slope of the absorbance of DPBF (418 nm) vs irradiation time

F: absorption correction factor,
F = 1 −
10
− OD
(OD: the optical density of L-CNDs and MB at 630 nm)

A thermal infrared imaging system was used to assess the photothermal conversion performance of the L-CNDs at 0.6 W·cm−2 808 nm. L-CNDs water solutions with varying concentrations (0–150 μg·mL−1) were put into EP tubes. The tubes were then treated with a 0.6 W·cm−2 808 nm laser, and temperature changes were observed using a thermal imaging equipment. The photothermal conversion efficiency η, was estimated using a 100 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs solution.

Aβ 42 (20 μM) were treated with varying doses of L-CNDs. Following 1 d of incubation, the fluorescence intensity of each Aβ 42 sample was assessed using fluorescence spectrophotometer set to 280 nm excitation. The spectra were recorded in the region 290–340 nm.

The ThT fluorescence test was utilized to investigate the inhibitory efficacy of L-CNDs against Aβ 42 fibrillation. pH=7.4 Tris-HCl buffer solution containing 10 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs was incubated with pre-treated Aβ 42 (20 μM). To identify Aβ 42 fibrillation, ThT (20 μM) was added to each sample. Using excitation at 440 nm, the ThT fluorescence at 485 nm was recorded at different times.

Using a Chirascan spectropolarimeter at 25 ℃ with a 10-mm route length, the CD spectra of Aβ (20 μM) treated with L-CNDs were observed. With a 1.0-nm bandwidth, the trials were conducted at wavelengths ranging 190–260 nm. The spectra of each sample were averaged over three scans.

PC12 and HT22 cells (1×104/well) were seeded in a 96-well plate. After the PC12/HT22 cells attained 75 % confluence, the culture medium was removed, and different concentrations of L-CNDs solution were applied. Each well contained 200 μL of L-CNDs solution, which was meticulously accounted for. The cells were cultivated for 1 d, additionally. Following incubation, the culture medium was carefully removed, and any remaining L-CNDs were removed from the cells by washing twice with PBS. Each well was then filled with the Cell-Counting Kit (CCK)-8 reagent, and after 2 h of incubation, the absorbance at 450 nm was determined. To examine how L-CNDs-remediating proteins caused cytotoxicity, Aβ 42 (20 μM) and L-CNDs at different doses were added. Cells were cultured for 24 h after ensuring a uniform quantity of 200 μL in each well. Following this, the culture media was carefully removed, and to remove any remaining impurities, the cells were rinsed twice with PBS. Following the addition of CCK-8 reagent to each well, the absorbance at 450 nm was measured after 2 h of incubation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Model of blood-brain barrier in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The approach for creating a double-layer blood-brain barrier was as follows: The coated Transwell was initially inoculated at a density of 5×105/mL using a rat tail collagen/gelatin-coated chamber. The top chamber was inoculated with 500 μL, and 1.5 mL DMEM was introduced to the bottom chamber to keep the liquid levels inside and outside the Transwell chamber level. The medium was replaced every 2 d. Starting on the 4th d after inoculation, when the culture media was fed to the upper and lower chambers every 2 d, a liquid level difference of 500 μL was developed, indicating that the cells had grown enough tightly. The lower chamber was inoculated with HT22 cells at a density of 70–80 %. L-CNDs were added to the top chamber after HT22 cells were affixed to the walls.

The Animal Care and Use Committee of the Air Force Medical University approved the acquisition of male KM mice at 8 weeks of age. The National Institutes of Health issued rules for the care and use of laboratory animals, which were followed in all the animal studies. The mice received an exact concentration of L-CND solution intravenously, whereas the control group received a similar amount of physiological saline through the tail vein. The injections were administered once daily for 7 d. The heart, liver, spleen, lungs, kidneys, and other organs were removed after the patient was asleep and were carefully cleaned using physiological saline. The organs were sectioned into approximately 3-μm thick sections, immersed in paraffin, and preserved in 4 % paraformaldehyde solution. The standard hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) method was used to stain the tissue slices. Finally, microscopic examinations were performed to further investigate the stained slices.

The L-CNDs were prepared using o-Phenylenediamine and l-glutamic acid as precursors via a solvent-thermal method. The L-CNDs exhibited a uniform distribution with no significant aggregation, as shown in the TEM image ( Fig. 1a). Supplementary Fig. S1 offers TEM images of L-CNDs at various resolution ration. Fig. 1b shows an average particle size of approximately 8.5 nm for the L-CNDs. The AFM image shows that the height of L-CNDs ranged 7.0–8.0 nm, indicating a spherical shape for L-CNDs (Fig. 1d). X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS) was used to examine the structure and elemental content of the L-CNDs. Three characteristic peaks were observed in the XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively, at 285, 400, and 531 eV, respectively (Fig. 1e). The C1s band is divided into three binding energy peaks at 284.4, 286.3, and 287.4 eV, which correspond to CC/C-C, C-N/C-O, and CO, respectively (Fig. 1f). The O1s spectra shows two peaks at 531.1 and 533.1 eV, representing the CO and C-O groups, respectively (Fig. 1g). The N1s spectra shows three peaks at 398.4, 399.3, and 400.4 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively (Fig. 1h) [33]. Fig. 1i shows the ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) absorption spectra of the L-CNDs, revealing distinct absorption peaks at 535, 578, and 630 nm. which belongs to the molecular-state region [37]. Subsequently, the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed to investigate the functional groups on the borders and surfaces of the L-CNDs (Fig. 1j), as well as those on C-O (1132 cm−1), C-N (1301–1349 cm−1), CC (1472 and 1501 cm−1), and CO/CN (1577 and 1618 cm−1) [38]. The L-CNDs were mostly composed of polyaromatic structures with functional oxygen and nitrogen groups according to the vibration absorption of the bonds. Raman spectroscopy revealed the presence of graphene-like structures in the L-CNDs (Fig. 1k). Characteristic D and G bands are observed in the peak spectrum, located at approximately 1335 and 1583 cm−1, respectively. The intensity ratio of these two peaks, approximately 0.92 (ID/IG) indicated that the L-CNDs were composed of a combination of graphene-like structures and other disordered structures [39]. The stability of the material was assessed by observing and characterizing the states of the L-CNDs in PBS using transmission electron microscopy at various time intervals (Supplementary Fig. S2). These findings indicated that the material exhibited excellent stability.

Using 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), L-CNDs' photocatalytic potential of the L-CNDs was assessed [40]. To determine how the DPBF Vis absorption peak of DPBF changed, various quantities of L-CNDs were added to the solution. The combined mixture was then continuously exposed to a 630 nm laser for 9 min. The generated singlet oxygen (1O2) causes chromogenic DPBF to undergo oxidation, resulting in the formation of colorless DPBF cation-free radicals. This process led to a decrease in the absorption intensity of DPBF at a wavelength of 418 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum. The findings presented in Fig. 2 show that L-CNDs had a powerful quenching impact on DPBF fluorescence at four distinct concentrations: 5 μg·mL−1, 10 μg·mL−1, and 20 μg·mL−1. The stronger the quenching effect, the higher the concentration. The quenching capacity of the L-CNDs was indicated by the change in the absorbance of DPBF at 418 nm. A better quenching effect was attained in 9 min (Fig. 2d), demonstrating the photodynamic characteristics of the L-CNDs. The free radicals generated were further confirmed by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR). To confirm the production of radical species, 4-amino-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) as a trap was utilized to capture 1O2 with short-lived to generate comparatively long-lived adducts. As shown in Fig. 2e, the L-CNDs showed no clear signals under dark conditions. However, after 6 min of illumination, the L-CNDs produced clear EPR signals, indicating the generation of 1O2. Using methylene blue (MB) as a standard, the 1O2 quantum yield (φ∆) could be calculated. As shown in Supplementary Fig. S3, the singlet oxygen quantum yield of L-CNDs is calculated to be 0.28. Compared with previous research results, it has a higher singlet oxygen quantum yield [41]. Photocatalysis can cause oxidation of protein residues, which in turn leads to the weakening of the connection between peptides.

The photothermal performance of the L-CNDs was characterized using an 808 nm excitation source and a thermal imaging camera. Upon irradiation with a power density of 0.6 W·cm−2 and a wavelength of 808 nm for 10 min, the temperature of a 5 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs solution gradually increased from an initial temperature of 28.2–31.6 °C. At a concentration of 50 μg·mL−1, the temperature increased from 28.8 to 37.3 °C. Upon increasing the concentration to 100 μg·mL−1, the temperature increased from 28.7 to 41.6 °C. When the concentration reached 150 μg·mL−1, the temperature rapidly increased from 28.4 to 45.7 °C (Fig. 2f). These results indicate that the L-CNDs exhibited excellent photothermal effects, with a clear concentration dependence of the temperature increase within a certain time frame. As shown in Fig. 2g, four heating and natural cooling cycles were performed on 100 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs using a laser with a power density of 0.6 W·cm−2 and a wavelength of 808 nm, demonstrating the good photothermal stability of L-CNDs. As shown in Fig. 2h and i, a 10-min irradiation of a 100 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs solution was followed by natural cooling. Based on the cooling curve, the photothermal conversion efficiency was calculated to be 68.25 %. Photothermal effect can disrupt the structure of Aβ, impede the aggregation of Aβ, and expedite the disaggregation process. Photothermal effects can alter the permeability of the blood-brain barrier, allowing nanomaterials to pass through. We evaluated the excitation-emission matrix fluorescence spectra of the L-CNDs with an excitation range of 200–900 nm and an emission range of 200–900 nm to investigate their fluorescence characteristics. The excitation-emission matrix fluorescence spectra (Supplementary Fig. S4a) demonstrate the two-photon excitation and emission properties of L-CNDs. The photoluminescence emissions of the L-CNDs solutions at various excitation wavelengths are shown in Supplementary Fig. S4b. The emission wavelength of the L-CNDs remained constant as the excitation wavelength changed, indicating that the luminescence was excitation-independent. The exceptional fluorescent qualities of L-CNDs have additional applications in the bioimaging domain.

To examine L-CNDs photooxidation of amyloid, we used the 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine (DNPH) colorimetric technique to detect the change in the carbonyl content of lysozyme amyloid (an AD model protein) following photooxidation. DNPH interacts with oxidized polypeptide carbonyl groups and has an absorption peak at 360 nm [42]. As shown in Fig. 3a, lysozyme alone exhibited a lower absorbance at 360 nm. The incubation of fresh Lys monomers with photoactivated L-CNDs resulted in a pronounced maximum peak at 360 nm, indicating an increase in the carbonyl content and oxygenation of Lys peptides by the photocatalytic interaction of L-CNDs with O2.

Tyrosine fluorescence, the endogenous fluorescence of Aβ 42, may be used to evaluate Aβ 42 adsorption or binding by measuring the degree of quenching[43]. Fig. 3b and c show that when the concentration of L-CNDs increased, the endogenous fluorescence of tyrosine at 303 nm decreased. This suggests that L-CNDs bind Aβ by hydrophobic interaction and π-π stacking. This finding supports the affinity of L-CNDs for Aβ 42.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aβ 42 aggregation inhibition and fibrils disaggregation
ce:section.ce:para: The aggregation process of Aβ 42 was tracked using ThT fluorescence staining. As shown in Supplementary Fig. S5a, the ThT fluorescence intensity of Aβ 42 protein slowly increases (0–1 h) during the initial incubation period, followed by a rapid increase (2–8 h), and then reaches a stable level (10–24 h). The aggregation process of Aβ 42 protein exhibits a sigmoidal growth curve, as depicted in Supplementary Fig. S5b. After 12 h of incubation, the ThT fluorescence signal at 484 nm remained relatively constant, indicating mature amyloid fibril formation. Supplementary Fig. S5c illustrates that the unaggregated Aβ 42 displayed two characteristic negative peaks at approximately 208 and 222 nm, representing the α-helical structure. During incubation, the negative peak at 208 nm gradually weakened. After 1 h of incubation, the negative peak at 208 nm disappeared, and positive and negative peaks appeared at approximately 200 and 219 nm, respectively. These peaks indicate an increase in the content of random coil and β-sheet structures. With increasing incubation time, the intensities of the peaks at approximately 200 and 219 nm increased. HRTEM image and element mapping images of Aβ 42 fibrils also show that fibrils cultivation is successful (Supplementary Fig. S6). These results provide strong evidence that the secondary structure of Aβ 42 undergoes a misfolding transition during the aggregation process, shifting from predominantly α-helical to β-sheet structures.

The L-CNDs were found to generate singlet oxygen under 630 nm irradiation. After treatment with 630 nm light, the inhibitory efficiency of Aβ 42 aggregation is enhanced by 62.38 % compared to the drug-only group (Supplementary Fig. S7a). Circular dichroism spectroscopy also demonstrates that L-CNDs could enhance the suppressant of Aβ 42 structural misfolding under PDT (Supplementary Fig. S7b). Similarly, the inhibitory efficiency was enhanced after treatment with the 808 nm laser compared to that in the drug-only group (Supplementary Fig. S8a). Circular dichroism spectroscopy results also confirmed that L-CNDs could enhance the suppressant of Aβ 42 structural misfolding under PTT (Supplementary Fig. S8b). Furthermore, the synergistic effects of PDT and PTT using L-CNDs were investigated to determine whether they were superior to PDT or PTT alone. As shown in Fig. 4a, the ThT fluorescence intensity in the group exposed to both 808 and 630 nm irradiation was significantly lower than that in the PTT and PDT groups. The inhibitory efficiencies of the L-CNDs under PTT, PDT, and PDT/PTT conditions were 61.08 %, 75.09 %, and 91.72 %, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4b, in the control group without L-CNDs, following a 24-h incubation of Aβ 42, a positive peak developed at 200 nm and a negative peak appeared at 218 nm, indicating the presence of a large amount of β-sheet structure. After applying 10 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs with 808 nm irradiation, the negative peak approximately 218 nm is no longer observed, and a new negative peak appear approximately 222 nm, corresponding to the α-helical structure of Aβ. Similar results were obtained following irradiation at 630 nm. As shown in Fig. 4c, large, intertwined, and thick amyloid fibrils, with an average length of approximately 590 nm, were formed in the control group after 24 h of incubation. When Aβ 42 was co-cultured with 10 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs and subjected to 808 nm irradiation, few branched fibrils are observed, and the fibrils length is approximately 390 nm. After 630 nm irradiation, the size and density of the fibrils in the sample further decreased, with fibril lengths as low as 170 nm. After irradiation at 808 and 630 nm, the fibril length decreased to 50 nm. These results indicate that L-CNDs significantly inhibit Aβ 42 aggregation under the synergistic effect of PTT and PDT, efficiently suppressing the misfolding of Aβ into β-sheet structures.

The effect of L-CNDs on disaggregating Aβ 42 aggregates is being investigated further. As shown in Fig. 4d, compared with the drug-only group, the light-treated group exhibited a significant decrease in ThT fluorescence intensity. The lowest ThT fluorescence intensity was observed with the synergistic effect of PDT and PTT, indicating that L-CNDs have a superior ability to disaggregate amyloid fibrils when assisted by PDT and PTT. Circular dichroism spectroscopy results show that the negative peak approximately 218 nm remains in the control group without L-CNDs after 48 h of incubation, indicating the presence of a large amount of β-sheet structure in the Aβ fibrils, maintaining their original conformation. When 10 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs were added to the Aβ fibrils, the intensity of the negative peak approximately 218 nm decreased, indicating a reduction in the β-sheet structure content. After irradiation treatment, a new negative peak appears approximately 222 nm, indicating an increase in the α-helical structure content. This result demonstrates the efficient disaggregation of Aβ fibrils by L-CNDs under PDT/PTT and the disruption of their β-sheet structure (Fig. 4e). As shown in Fig. 4f, after 24 h of incubation, the control group maintained densely packed and intertwined amyloid fibrils with an average length of approximately 620 nm. When Aβ 42 fibrils were co-cultured with 10 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs, longer amyloid fibrils with lengths approximately 460 nm were observed. After 808 nm irradiation, the fibril length decreased to approximately 270 nm. After irradiation at 630 nm, fibril length decreased to approximately 160 nm. After irradiation at 808 and 630 nm, fibril length decreased to approximately 100 nm. These results indicate that under the PDT and PTT synergistic effect of L-CNDs, Aβ 42 aggregates can be significantly disaggregated. Much research concentrates on employing carbon nanodots as nanocarriers to transport pharmaceuticals for medicinal purposes. This study investigates how the optical features of carbon nanodots may be used in amyloid illness [44,45]. The microenvironment in the brains of Alzheimer's disease patients is hypoxic, and photothermal activity may enhance blood flow, thus increasing oxygen supply. In comparison to earlier study, 10 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs demonstrate superior inhibition and disaggregation effects at a reduced dosage [46]. In future in vivo trials, if the generation of 1O2 is inadequate to oxidize Aβ owing to the anoxic microenvironment, the dosage of L-CNDs will be increased while maintaining minimal toxicity to normal cells and tissues.

A CCK-8 assay was used to assess the cytotoxicity of L-CNDs against PC12 and HT22 cells. Exposing PC12 cells to 200 μg·mL−1 of L-CNDs maintains their viability at 86.1 % under dark circumstances. Using a 630 + 808 nm laser, cell viability is roughly 82.9 % at 200 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs (Supplementary Fig. S9a). HT22 cells maintain 83.0 % vitality at 200 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs in the absence of laser. After irradiation, the cytotoxicity of 200 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs on HT22 cells is roughly 80.3 % (Supplementary Fig. S9b). These findings provide solid evidence of the minimal cytotoxicity of L-CNDs toward PC12 and HT22 cells.

The essential organs of the rats, including the heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys, were extracted after a 7-d intravenous injection of physiological saline and a solution of L-CNDs at a dose of 1 mg·kg−1. Further histological analysis using H&E staining demonstrated a lack of clear tissue inflammation, structural impairment, or any other abnormal modifications in the L-CND-treated tissues compared to the control group (Supplementary Fig. S10). These findings support the remarkable biocompatibility of the L-CNDs, indicating their potential relevance in in vivo studies.

Using the CCK-8 assay, it was possible to assess how well L-CNDs mitigated the cytotoxicity caused by Aβ 42. The cell viability of PC12 cells is 73.7 % following treatment with 20 μM Aβ 42, as seen in Fig. 5a. Under light irradiation, the cell viability increases to 84.5 % and 95.0 % with 5 μg·mL−1 and 10 μg·mL−1, respectively. Without light irradiation, the PC12 cell viability is 82.4 % with 10 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs. With 808 nm irradiation, PC12 cell viability increased to 86.0 %, and with 630 nm irradiation, PC12 cell viability increased to 91.0 %. When both light irradiations were applied, the PC12 cell viability increased to 95.0 % (Fig. 5b). As shown in Fig. 5c, after treatment with 20 μM Aβ 42, the cell viability of HT22 cells is 79.6 %. Under light irradiation, the cell viability increases to 85.2 % and 88.5 % with 5 μg·mL−1 and 10 μg·mL−1, respectively. Without irradiation, the HT22 cell viability is 81.7 % with 10 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs. With 808 nm irradiation, HT22 cell viability increased to 83.8 %, and with 630 nm irradiation, HT22 cell viability increased to 86.7 %. When both light irradiations were applied, the HT22 cell viability increased to 88.5 % (Fig. 5d). The above results indicate that L-CNDs possess excellent capabilities in alleviating Aβ 42-induced cytotoxicity. The excitation wavelength of L-CNDs is greater than that of OPCDs documented in the literature, and it has a superior capacity to alleviate Aβ-induced cytotoxicity [47].

Using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM), the inhibitory effect of L-CNDs on Aβ 42 fibrils binding to cells was examined. ThT staining is used to visualize amyloid fibrils and provides a green fluorescent signal [48]. In the control group, HT22 cells co-incubated with Aβ 42 for 24 h showed a strong ThT fluorescence signal, indicating the presence of numerous amyloid fibrils. The group treated with 10 μg·mL−1 L-CNDs showed decreased ThT fluorescence and plaque size. In the groups subjected to 630 and 808 nm irradiation, the disappearance of green fluorescence around the cells was clearly observed (Fig. 5e). These results indicate that under irradiation, amyloid plaques significantly decrease in size and the binding between amyloid plaques and cells weakens, protecting cells from damage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The potential of L-CNDs to break through the BBB
ce:section.ce:para: The therapeutic effectiveness of numerous drug molecules is restricted by the presence of the blood-brain barrier (BBB), which poses a challenge to their penetration through this protective barrier [49,50]. The primary constituents of the BBB are the endothelial cells, which play vital roles in controlling the transport of substances into the brain. bEnd.3 cells were selected, and flow cytometry and CLSM were performed.

The fluorescence intensity emitted by the L-CNDs is represented on the x-axis in Fig. 6a, whereas the number of cells is represented on the y-axis. Without medication, the fluorescence intensity of the control group was normalized to zero. After drug administration, the peak shifted to the right, indicating an increase in fluorescence intensity and slow uptake of L-CNDs by the cells. The longer the L-CNDs were exposed, the more they entered the cells. After 8 h of treatment, the cellular absorption rate of the L-CNDs was 84.6 %. CLSM was used to observe L-CND absorption by bEnd.3 cells in an in vitro context, as seen in Fig. 6b. The blue fluorescence signal from DAPI staining indicates the cell nucleus, whereas the green fluorescence signal from Dio staining indicates the cell membrane. The red fluorescence signal indicates the presence of L-CNDs. After 0.5 h of treatment, a modest red fluorescent signal appeared between the cell nucleus and the cell membrane. After 8 h of drug delivery, a significant increase in red fluorescence was observed within the cells, which was consistent with the data obtained from flow cytometry analysis. These findings show that L-CNDs entered bEnd.3 cells via endocytosis. Collectively, these findings indicate the promising potential of L-CNDs in overcoming the BBB and their immense potential in the diagnosis and treatment of AD.

To confirm the delivery of L-CNDs to the brain, a cell co-culturing immortalized brain-derived endothelial cells.3 and mouse hippocampal neuronal cell line (bEnd.3 and HT22) in vitro model was established ( Fig. 7a). The absorbance of the L-CNDs showed a good linear relationship with correlation coefficients (R2) greater than 0.999 (Fig. 7b). A certain amount of medium from the bottom chamber was collected at different time points, and the fluorescence intensity was analyzed. Fig. 7c shows the increase in the transport ratio after treatment with L-CNDs over the incubation time. After 2 h of incubation, the transport ratio was 10.61 %. After 8 h of incubation, the transport ratio was calculated to be 53.02 %. The flow cytometry results are shown in Fig. 7d. Without L-CNDs, the fluorescence intensity of the control group normalized to zero. L-CNDs were found in 93.9 % of the HT22 cells after 8 h of treatment. Similar findings were obtained by fluorescence confocal microscopy of HT22 cells (Fig. 7e). Endothelial cells are connected by tight junctions, which include openings that enable L-CNDs to pass through via paracellular transport. In addition, endocytosis facilitated the penetration of L-CNDs into bEnd.3 cells. One possible explanation is that the presence of glutamate transporters in the cell membrane of endothelial cells, together with the preservation of the glutamate structure at carbon dots, may contribute to this phenomenon. These results suggest that the L-CNDs have a greater ability to penetrate the BBB. HT22 cells showed a high capacity to internalize L-CNDs, indicating that L-CNDs may effectively operate inside and outside the cell.

In this study, light-responsive carbon nanodots (L-CNDs) were prepared by a solvothermal method. L-CNDs' hydrophobic core and large conjugated domain limit Aβ 42 aggregation and promote aggregate disaggregation via hydrophobic interactions and π-π stacking. The L-CNDs efficiently generated 1O2 under 630 nm irradiation and exhibited good photothermal conversion efficiency under 808 nm irradiation, enabling synergistic PDT and PTT. After 630 nm irradiation, the inhibition efficiency was significantly enhanced, showing a 62.38 % improvement compared to the no-light group. The L-CNDs respond to 808 nm near-infrared light and achieve a photothermal conversion efficiency of 68.25 %. At a concentration of 10 μg·mL−1, L-CNDs exhibit inhibition efficiencies of 61.08 %, 75.09 %, and 91.72 % under PTT, PDT, and PDT/PTT, respectively, against Aβ aggregation. L-CNDs effectively inhibit the misfolding of Aβ, efficiently disaggregate Aβ fibrils, and disrupt their β-sheet structures under PDT/PTT. Furthermore, L-CNDs demonstrate low cytotoxicity toward PC12 and HT22 cells and alleviate Aβ-induced cellular toxicity. Integrating different therapy, using reduced dosages of photosensitizers, or applying lower intensity light exposure may enhance Aβ clearance while reducing toxicity to normal tissues. The L-CNDs also exhibited excellent photoluminescence properties, good biocompatibility, and small size, facilitating BBB penetration. The results of this study provide valuable insights into the treatment of AD.

Xu Shao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Muqiong Li: Software, Methodology, Formal analysis. Chaoren Yan: Writing – review & editing, Software, Methodology. Chao Wang: Writing – review & editing, Software, Methodology. Xin Wang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis. Ping Guan: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis. Xiaoling Hu: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Li Fan: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Xu Shao and Muqiong Li contributed equally to this study.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (82360368). This work was financially supported by the Key Research and Development Program of Shaanxi (2020GY-285, 2022GY-198, and 2023-YBGY-295) and Natural Science Basic Research Program of Shaanxi Province (2023-JC-YB-668).
